coredata.dc:title: Portable bifunctional kits for polychromatic visual assay of tetracycline and copper(II)

coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Yingqi
Zhang, Lina
Xu, Jun
Zhao, Xinna
Bi, Ning
Li, Yongxin
Jia, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Contamination from tetracycline (TC) and copper ions (Cu2+) poses a significant threat to aquatic ecosystems and human health. Developing an efficient platform to achieve ultrasensitive and on-site synchronous detection of TC and Cu2+ is urgently needed. Herein, a dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe (Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF) was elaborately designed based on europium ions (Eu3+) decorated green-fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) cooperating with red-fluorescent Eu-MOF. This bifunctional nanoprobe exhibited specific response to Cu2+ in the 275 nm channel, and special recognition ability for TC in the 385 nm channel due to the “antenna effect”. Ultrasensitive cross-interference-free detection of TC and Cu2+ were realized with the LOD of 3.70 and 6.13 nM, respectively. Satisfactory results were achieved in various real samples detection. Moreover, smartphone-assisted portable paper- and hydrogel kit-based sensors were constructed for fast and on-site visual assay of TC and Cu2+. This study provided an effective method for ultrasensitive and simultaneous detection of TC and Cu2+, and offered some references for the construction of portable and multifunctional nanosensors for facile use in daily life.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Portable nanosensor
Dual-channel fluorescent detection
Carbon dots
Tetracycline
Copper(II)
Europium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs)
Dual-channel ratiometric FL detection of Cu2+ and TC
Paper- and hydrogel kit-based sensors for visualization detection of Cu2+ and TC
Detection of Cu2+ and TC in real samples by Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF
Dual-channel ratiometric FL detection of Cu2+ and TC
Sensing mechanism of the dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe
Applications of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF in practical sample detection
Intelligent visual detection application of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the foundation of the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province ( 242300421247 ), the Science and Technology Innovation Team in Henan Universities ( 24IRTSTHN014 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51773052 , U1904168 , 21805071 ), and the Key Scientific Research Project of Henan Province ( 24A150011 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CDs. (b) SEM, and (c) TEM images of Eu-MOF. (d) TEM image, (e) XRD spectra, (f) FTIR spectra, and (g) Full-scan XPS spectrum of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF. High-resolution N 1 s spectra of CDs (h), Eu-MOF (i), and CDs@Eu-MOF (j).

FL spectra of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF under various amounts of Cu2+ (λex = 275 nm) (a), and TC (λex = 385 nm) (e). Fitting plot of the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I616/I525) and Cu2+ concentration (b), and TC concentration (f). CIE coordinate plots in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ (c), and TC (g). Corresponding FL color changes of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF under 254 nm UV lamp (d), and 365 nm UV light (h).

UV–visible absorption spectra of the nanoprobe in the absence and presence of Cu2+ (a), and TC (e). Energy transfer process in Eu-MOF under the excitation of 275 nm (b), and in Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF at 385 nm excitation (f). FL spectra of the nanoprobe in the absence and presence of Cu2+ (c), and TC (g). FL lifetime decay curve of Eu3+ in Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF with and without Cu2+ addition (λex =275 nm) (d), and TC addition (λex =385 nm) (h).

(a) Impact of interference species on nanoprobe sensing of TC and Cu2+, with FL color change photos (10 μM each). (b) Test paper-based nanosensor construction, color response to Cu2+ and TC, and concentration of Cu2+ and TC versus R/G plots via smartphone app. (c) Portable Jelly powder-based kits for visual detection, and smartphone-aided schematic for Cu2+ and TC intelligent detection.
Diagrammatic drawing for the synthesis of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF and sensing towards TC and Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Portable bifunctional kits for polychromatic visual assay of tetracycline and copper(II)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Environmental Monitoring Technology, Henan Ecological Environment Monitoring and Safety Center, Zhengzhou, Henan 450046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Environmental Monitoring Technology
Henan Ecological Environment Monitoring and Safety Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Environmental Monitoring Technology, Henan Ecological Environment Monitoring and Safety Center, Zhengzhou, Henan, 450046, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Contamination from tetracycline (TC) and copper ions (Cu2+) poses a significant threat to aquatic ecosystems and human health. Developing an efficient platform to achieve ultrasensitive and on-site synchronous detection of TC and Cu2+ is urgently needed. Herein, a dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe (Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF) was elaborately designed based on europium ions (Eu3+) decorated green-fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) cooperating with red-fluorescent Eu-MOF. This bifunctional nanoprobe exhibited specific response to Cu2+ in the 275 nm channel, and special recognition ability for TC in the 385 nm channel due to the “antenna effect”. Ultrasensitive cross-interference-free detection of TC and Cu2+ were realized with the LOD of 3.70 and 6.13 nM, respectively. Satisfactory results were achieved in various real samples detection. Moreover, smartphone-assisted portable paper- and hydrogel kit-based sensors were constructed for fast and on-site visual assay of TC and Cu2+. This study provided an effective method for ultrasensitive and simultaneous detection of TC and Cu2+, and offered some references for the construction of portable and multifunctional nanosensors for facile use in daily life.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC), known for its low cost and high antibacterial activity, is extensively used not only as a broad-spectrum antibiotic for human and veterinary therapy, but also as a growth promoter in livestock and aquaculture [1]. However, more than 75 % of TC cannot be absorbed by organisms and is excreted in its active form through feces and urine [2–4], resulting in serious damage to aquatic ecosystems. Moreover, the TC residues in aquatic products and animal-derived foods can continuously accumulate in the human body through the food chain, generating serious threat to human health, involving superbacteria, fatty liver, gastrointestinal disorders and pancreatitis [1,5–8].

Besides, copper ion (Cu2+) which is extensively used in nuclear industry [9], agricultural [10] and metallurgy activities [11], is becoming to be the uppermost heavy–metal toxin, especially in natural and drinking waters. Excessive Cu2+ can be accumulated in organisms through drinking water or food chains, leading to enormous risk for human health, such as cancer, Alzheimer’s disease [12], leukemia, cirrhosis [11], and so on. In view of the high toxicity and bioaccumulation effects of TC and Cu2+, the safety limits of TC in milk and Cu2+ in drinkable water are stipulated by the European Union and US EPA as 225 nM and 20 μM, respectively. Developing an efficient platform capable of ultrasensitive, on-site, and simultaneous detection of TC and Cu2+ is of immense significance to ecological security and human health.

Hitherto multitudinous analysis techniques have been developed for TC or Cu2+ inspection, involving HPLC, capillary electrophoresis [13], ICP spectroscopy, atomic absorption spectroscopy [14], and so forth. Nevertheless, these traditional techniques usually rely on expensive and cumbersome instrument, tedious sample handling procedures and professional personnels, representing a huge obstacle for facile, rapid and on site practical applications. Amongthepotentialstrategies to overcome this barrier, fluorometry stands out due to its rapidity, simplification, visualization, low-price and high sensitivity. In particular, the ratiometric fluorescence (FL) strategy featuring two self-calibrated emission peaks [15] not only yields more accurate results by mitigating the impact of environmental or instrumental factors, but also facilitates visual detection of targets through the dose-sensitive colorimetric response of the fluorescent probe [16–18].

Of late, miscellaneous fluorescent materials have been utilized to construct ratiometric FL probe for TC or Cu2+ detection [8,15,19,20]. However, the majority of these probes are unable to achieve simultaneous detection of both TC and Cu2+. Notably, multiplex detection stands out as an effective strategy for simultaneously detecting multiple targets, boasting advantages of high efficiency, cost-effectiveness and reliability. Despite the existence of several nanoprobes capable of concurrent TC and Cu2+ detection [21,22], they grapple with key issues such as low sensitivity, non-visual inspection, and lack of portability. Consequently, there is a pressing need for a bifunctional fluorescent sensing platform to achieve simultaneous, ultrasensitive and fast visual detecting of TC and Cu2+.

Benefiting from the high photoluminescence efficiency, narrowband emissions, large stokes shift and good photostability, lanthanide-based fluorescent materials, especially Eu3+ with red emission and Tb3+ with green emission, are outstanding candidates for multicolor nanoprobe, which have great potential in visualization inspection [7]. Moreover, Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions are also ideal choices for the construction of multifunctional hybrid materials due to that their FL emission can not only be induced by specific organic ligands but also be regulated by the excitation wavelength, endowing them with the potential of multivariate recognition under different excitation channels. For instance, 2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) can effectively transfer energy to Eu3+, resulting intense red emission of Eu3+ under 254 nm excitation [23], while oxytetracycline can induce red emission of Eu3+ under 365 nm excitation [24]. In addition, the red emission of Eu3+ can also be quenched by other species, such as heavy metal ions [25], providing possibility of dual-channel detection of multiple substrates.

Based on the above consideration, a dual-channel ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF was carefully designed and facilely constructed based on Eu3+ decorated green-fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) cooperating with red-fluorescent Eu-MOF (Scheme 1 ). Compared to the currently reported single- or dual-function fluorescent probes for TC and/or Cu2+ detection, this facilely synthesized nanoprobe exhibited notable advantages. It not only enabled synchronous, cross-interference-free and multicolor visual detection of Cu2+ and TC in dual channels but also exhibited ultra-high detection sensitivity for both analytes. Furthermore, the construction of smartphone-aided portable hydrogel kit-based sensors not only facilitated rapid and on-site visual assay of Cu2+ and TC, but also allowed for high-throughput simultaneous detection of various samples. Satisfactory results have been achieved by using this strategy for the inspection of Cu2+ and TC in multiple actual samples, presenting promising prospect in real-time monitoring of aquatic ecosystems and food security.

2,6-pyridine dicarboxylic acid (DPA), pamoic acid and urea were obtained from Adamas Reagents Ltd. Eu(NO3)3⋅6H2O (99.99 %) was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. Tetracycline (TC) was purchased from Xiya Reagent Co. Copper chloride (CuCl2) was purchased from Tianjin Kaixin Chemical Industry Co. Fish (Pomfret), shrimp (Qingdao prawns), oyster (Pacific oyster) and kelp were purchased from Lubao Vegetable Market of Jiaozuo. Jelly powder was purchased from Dongguan Forrest Food Technology Co.

The microstructure of the materials was characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL2100). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were collected by a EscaLab 250Xi X-ray diffractometer within the range of 5–80°. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Bruker VERTEX 70 FTIR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray Electron spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra measures were performed on a Prism F97 Pro fluorescence spectrometer. Fluorescence lifetimes were collected on a fluorescence phosphorescence spectrometer (FLS1000). UV–visible absorption spectra were collected by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Youke). The photographs were taken using an iPhone 13 Pro Max smartphone, and the software used to analyze the RGB values was FCPicker.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Pamoic acid (350 mg) and urea (54 mg) were dispersed in deionized water (30 mL), respectively, which were then mixed and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, transfer the above mixture to a reactor and heat at 453 K for 10 h. When cooled to room temperature, the supernatant was separated by centrifugation, which was then placed in a dialysis bag of 500 MWCO and dialyzed in deionized water for 2 days. After freeze drying, CDs powder was collected.

A modified method was used to prepare the Eu-MOF [26]. 133.8 mg Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (0.3 mmol) and 125 mg DPA (0.3 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL anhydrous ethanol respectively, which were then sonicated for 10 min and mixed together. After ultrasound for 15 min, transfer the above solution to a 50 mL reactor and heated at 453 K for 72 h. Upon cooling to room temperature, white precipitate was obtained by centrifugation. After drying under vacuum at 323 K, the powdered Eu-MOF were collected and stored under seal at room temperature.

125 mg Eu-MOF and 25 mg CDs were dispersed in 10 mL anhydrous ethanol and refluxed at 333 K for 12 h to obtain the product CDs@Eu-MOF. Then 125 mg Eu(NO3)3·6H2O was dissolved in 2 mL anhydrous ethanol and added dropwise to the CDs@Eu-MOF mixture, which was then refluxed at 333 K for 24 h. After centrifugation, the precipitate was washed using ethanol and distilled water alternately. The product of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOFs was obtained after drying the precipitate under vacuum at 323 K. The powdered nanoprobe were collected into centrifuge tubes and stored under seal at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-channel ratiometric FL detection of Cu2+ and TC
ce:section.ce:para: 2 Mg Eu-CDs@Eu-MOFs was dispersed in 1 mL distilled water, then take 10 μL of the above solution and HEPES buffer solution (10 Μ, pH = 8.0) to make 2 mL mixed solution, which were further transferred to a 4 mL quartz cuvette. 10 μL different concentrations of TC solution was added to the above quartz cuvette. After mixing well, the FL spectra of the above solution were recorded under 385 nm excitation. As for the detection of Cu2+, except that different concentrations of Cu2+ solution was added and the excitation wavelength was 285 nm, the other procedures were the same as that of TC detection. The above method was also used for the detection of other interference species, which was repeated three times at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper- and hydrogel kit-based sensors for visualization detection of Cu2+ and TC
ce:section.ce:para: Paper-based nanosensor was fabricated as follow: 5 mg Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF was dispersed in 1 mL deionized water. The filter paper was cut into small round pieces with a radius of 0.5 cm by using a perforator, which was then immersed in the above Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF solution for 4 h. After that, the test paper loaded with Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF was taken out and dried naturally. Subsequently, the above test paper were immersed in different concentrations of TC or Cu2+ standard solutions for 10 min. After taking out and drying at room temperature, the discoloration of the paper was recorded using a smart phone under the ultraviolet lamp (365 or 254 nm), respectively.

To construct the hydrogel kit-based nanosensor, 10 mg Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF was dispersed in 2 mL deionized water, then 10 mL hot water and 30 mg jelly powder was successively added to the above solution under stirring. After mixing well, the above solution was dropped on the kit mold, naturally cooled and solidified. Then the prepared TC or Cu2+ solution was added to the hydrogel kit. After that, the color of the hydrogel was recorded using a smart phone under a 365 or 254 nm UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and TC in real samples by Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: Real water samples were collected from lakes around Jiaozuo City, which were pre-treated, centrifuged, and filtered to remove insoluble substances. The pretreatment method for actual fish, shrimp and oysters samples were slightly modified according to the reference method [27]. Then 10 g meat of the above mentioned seafood was taken chopped and homogenized by adding 2 mL deionized water, and then was processed according to the reference method for the clear solution to be tested [28]. The processing of vegetable sample involved homogenizing 5 g of dried kelp, followed by the addition of 5 mL of HNO3 for wet digestion. Subsequently, 5 mL of HNO3 was further added, and the solution was heated at 120 °C for 24 h. The above solution was then evaporated to dryness, and deionized water was added to adjust the final volume to 10 mL [29].

The microstructure of the materials were investigated by TEM and SEM measurements. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, well-dispersed spherical particles of CDs can be obviously visible, whose size distribution was between 0.5–2 nm, with a mean diameter of 1.07 nm. As delineated in the SEM images of Eu-MOF (Fig. 1b), a fascinating flower-like structure formed by petal-like stacking sheet crystals can be obviously visible. TEM characterization of the Eu-MOF (Fig. 1c) demonstrated its ‘petals’ were irregular lamellar crystals with diameters greater than 100 nm. Fig. 1d showed the TEM image of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF, in which can be observed that the lamellar crystals were intact and black dotted particles were dispersed on their surface. The particles sizes corresponded to that of CDs, which indicated that the CDs were successfully loaded on Eu-MOF, while the structure of Eu-MOF has not been damaged. This can also be verified by the XRD patterns of CDs, Eu-MOF, CDs@Eu-MOF and Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF (Fig. 1e). In the XRD of CDs, a broad peak at 25.4° can be observed [30], while the characteristic diffraction peaks of Eu-MOF were consistent with that reported by Wang et al. [31], which indicated the successful preparation of Eu-MOF. As portrayed in the XRD pattern of CDs@Eu-MOF and Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF (Fig. 1e), both the diffraction peaks of Eu-MOF and the broad characteristic peak of CDs appeared clearly, which indicated that the CDs were successfully attached on Eu-MOF, while the crystalline structure of Eu-MOF was still intact.

Furthermore, the surface functional groups of the materials were investigated by FTIR tests (Fig. 1f). The absorption peaks of CDs located at 1641, 2925 and 3410 cm−1 were induced by the stretching vibrations of –COOH, C-H, and –OH/–NH2, respectively [32], while that at 1507 cm−1 was originated from the bending vibration of –NH, suggesting that the surface of CDs were enriched with –COOH and –NH2, which provided abundant sites for the subsequent introduction of Eu3+. In the FTIR spectrum of Eu-MOF, the absorption bands at 3388, 3079 and 1618 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH, −C=C-H, and carboxyl respectively [33]. As for CDs@Eu-MOF, two peaks at 1510 and 1643 cm−1 corresponded to the bending and stretching vibration of –NH and –COOH can be visible in Fig. 1f, indicating the successful introduction of CDs in Eu-MOF. In addition, the –COOH in Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF was redshifted compared to that in CDs@Eu-MOF, indicating that the Eu3+ was coordinated with the carboxyl group in CDs@Eu-MOF to form the target composite Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF.

Moreover, the chemical composition of the materials were investigated by XPS measurement (Fig. 1g). The full-scan XPS spectra indicated that the prepared CDs@Eu-MOF contains C, O, N and Eu. In the N 1 s spectra of CDs (Fig. 1h), N mainly existed in the form of N-H (400.9 keV) and N-C (399.6 keV), which manifested that there were rich amino groups in CDs [34]. As portrayed in Fig. 1i, two peaks represented the existence of N-C (399.9 keV) and pyridine N (398.8 keV) in the N 1 s spectrum of Eu-MOF. Notably, Fig. 1j covered all forms of nitrogen on the surface of CDs and MOF, demonstrating the successful synthesis of CDs@Eu-MOF [35,36]. The high-resolution EDX spectra of the materials were shown in Fig. S1, and the elemental analysis of the EDX revealed the ratio of N to Eu in those materials. The ratio of N to Eu in CDs@Eu-MOF (4.14) was higher than that in Eu-MOF (4.01), due to the introduction of N-containing CDs to Eu-MOF, while the N to Eu ratio in Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF reduced to 3.76 because of the introduction of Eu3+, which verified the successful synthesis of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF.

To achieve the optimal sensing effect, the FL response conditions were optimized, including excitation wavelength, response time, buffer system, pH value and stability. As delineated in Fig. S2a and S2b, in the presence of 6 μM Cu2+ or TC, the maximum value of I616/I525 appeared at the excitation wavelength of 275 and 385 nm, respectively, under the condition that the emission peak of CDs was intact. As delineated in Fig. S3 and S4, the maximum change of FL intensity ratio (I616/I525) occurred in the HEPES buffer and pH = 8. Upon the addition of Cu2+ or TC, the FL intensity ratio (I616/I525) of the nanoprobe reached its maximum at 15 s and remained almost constant, indicating a rapid detection of the assay (Fig. S5). Fig. S6 indicated that Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF exhibited prominent photostability before and after adding Cu2+ or TC, which was of great significance for practical detection applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-channel ratiometric FL detection of Cu2+ and TC
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized experimental conditions, sensing performance of the probe towards Cu2+ and TC were investigated. For channel 1, under 275 nm excitation, the FL emission of Eu3+ located at 590, 616, 651 and 694 nm originating from Eu-MOF in the nanoprobe decreased as the concentration of Cu2+ increased, while the FL emission of CDs at 525 nm was nearly unchanged (Fig. 2 a). As portrayed in Fig. 2b, the FL intensity ratio I616/I525 of the probe showed an excellent functional relationship with Cu2+ within 0–17 μ M (R2 = 0.9962) and good linearity within 0–1.0 μ M (R2 = 0.9932). The LOD was determined to be 6.13 nM, which was superior to most of the reported literature (Table S1) and well below the maximum allowable concentration in drinkable water stipulated by U.S. EPA (20 μM) [37], indicating high sensitivity of the nanoprobe for trace detection of Cu2+. The CIE chromaticity coordinates of the nanoprobe indicated obvious changes from red (0.5242, 0.4021) to green (0.3287, 0.4675) (Fig. 2c, Table S2), which was consistent with the color transition of the sensing system under 254 nm UV lamp upon Cu2+ detection (Fig. 2d), providing the possibility of multicolor visualization detection of Cu2+.

For channel 2, under the excitation of 385 nm, the nanoprobe exhibited green FL from CDs at 525 nm. After the addition of TC, the red FL emission of Eu3+ on the surface of CDs was triggered and enhanced obviously with the increasing amount of TC (Fig. 2e). Fig. 2f displayed the linear plot of the FL intensity ratio (I616/I525) and TC concentration, and a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9988) within the range of 0–15 μM can be observed. The LOD was calculated to be 3.70 nM, which was much lower than the maximum allowable values stipulated by FDA (676 nM) and the European Union (225 nM) [38,39], and superior to most of the reported literature (Table S3). The CIE coordinates were displayed in Fig. 2g and Table S4. The corresponding color changes of the nanoprobe upon TC detection under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp were shown in Fig. 2h, which manifested a good prospect in multi-color visual detection of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of the dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the sensing mechanism of the nanoprobe toward Cu2+, series of investigation were carried out. From Fig. 3 a, it was evident that the fluorescent nanoprobe possessed two absorption bands located at 271 and 278 nm, in which the former was due to the π-π* transition of −C=C-H on Eu-MOF, and the latter originated from the pyridine ring [40,41]. The addition of Cu2+ shifted the former absorption peak to 270 nm, while the absorption peak at 278 nm showed a significant decrease, indicating the strong interaction of Cu2+ with the pyridine ring. As demonstrated in Fig. 3b, the red FL of Eu-MOF in the probe Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF came from.

the sensitization of Eu3+ by the ligand DPA in the MOF [42]. The 5D0→7F2 transition of Eu3+ caused a strong FL emission peak at 616 nm (Fig. 3c). When Cu2+ was present, Eu3+ coordinated with DPA in Eu-MOF were readily replaced by Cu2+, which had a stronger ability to coordinate with DPA to form DPA-Cu, blocking the sensitization effect of DPA to Eu3+. Thus the red FL disappeared, which was then revealed as the green FL emission of CDs [43]. In addition, the FL lifetime decay curves were collected (Fig. 3d), and the lifetime of Eu3+ in the nanoprobe decreased from 0.317 ms to 0.213 ms after adding Cu2+ according to the formula (1) and (2) (Table S5) [15], which further demonstrated the sensing mechanism of the nanoprobe towards Cu2+ was that Cu2+ replaced the Eu3+ coordinated with the DPA ligand, blocking the sensitization effect of DPA to Eu3+.
(1)

τ av

τ 2

To explore the sensing mechanism towards TC, following studies were performed. As delineated in Fig. 3e, TC exhibited an obvious absorption peak at 362 nm, whereas the absorption peak of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF + TC shifted to 390 nm, manifesting an interaction between TC and Eu3+ on the surface of CDs [20]. Fig. 3f demonstrated the energy transfer process from TC to Eu3+. TC can absorb high-energy photons of ultraviolet light and jumps from the ground state (S0) to the lowest excited singlet state (S1). Then the intersystem jump occurs to reach the triplet state energy level (T1), and then transfers the energy to the Eu3+, thereby sensitizes the luminescence of Eu3+ at 616 nm (5D0 → 7F2), 593 nm (5D0 → 7F1), 653 nm (5D0 → 7F3) and 697 nm (5D0 → 7F4), namely the so-called “antenna effect”. Furthermore, the reduction in the number of water molecules coordinated with Eu3+ upon the addition of TC is also a crucial factor contributing to the fluorescence enhancement of Eu3+. Before adding TC to the system, the FL of Eu3+ in the probe was quenched by the coordinated H2O molecules, and the nanoprobe displayed only green FL of CDs (Fig. 3g). After the introduction of 10 μ M TC, the coordinated H2O molecules were replayed by TC with diketone structure due to TC’s stronger coordination ability with Eu3+, which inhibited the nonradiative quenching of the H2O molecules [44] and prolonged the FL lifetime of Eu3+. Specifically, the FL lifetime of the probe increased from 7.390 μs to 22.840 μs upon adding TC (Fig. 3h, Table S6), further corroborating the changesin the coordination sphere of Eu3+.

As an important performance indicator of nanoprobes, the selectivity of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF upon Cu2+ and TC detection was investigated. As portrayed in Fig. 4 a, the nanoprobe had bright red FL under 254 nm UV light, and the FL intensity (Fig. S7) and color nearly unchanged after adding interfering species. However, when the same concentration of Cu2+ was added, the red FL was significantly quenched and the green FL appeared, manifesting prominent selectivity for Cu2+ detection. In addition, when interfering species coexisted, the response of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF to Cu2+ was almost unaffected, showing excellent anti-interference ability.

When the nanoprobe was under a 365 nm UV lamp, it emitted a bright green FL and remained unchanged after adding interfering species. When TC was present in the solution, significant discoloration of the sensing system can be observed from green to red, exhibiting outstanding selectivity and anti-interference ability for TC detection. More importantly, when TC and Cu2+ were present at the same time, the two-channel detection of the target analyte (TC or Cu2+) did not affect each other, which achieved cross-interference-free determination of TC and Cu2+ (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF in practical sample detection
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the feasibility of the nanoprobe, it was applied to the detection of actual samples. 2 mg Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF was dispersed in 1 mL deionized water. Then 10μL of the above mixture was added to 4 mL quartz cuvette, followed by the addition of HEPES buffer to make 2 mL solution. The processed actual sample (10 μL) was added to the cuvette, then a series of TC or Cu2+ with different concentrations were spiked, and the assay results obtained using the fluorescent probe were processed and calculated (Table S7 and S8). The results indicated that the recoveries of TC and Cu2+ were within the range of 90.64 %–117.7 % and 97.20–115.56 %, respectively, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) were basically within 5 %. It was demonstrated that Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF provided a reliable result for the assay of TC or Cu2+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intelligent visual detection application of Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve convenient, fast, and low-cost intelligent visual inspection, paper-based and hydrogel kit-based portable sensors were developed with the help of a smartphone color processing app.The surface roughness of the filter paper provides favorable conditions for Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF attachment, and the fabrication process is simple and fast (Fig. 4b). Different color changes occurred when the test paper was exposed to the target substance with different concentrations. Under 254 nm UV light, the color of the test paper transit from red to green in the presence of Cu2+. The relationship between G/R values and the concentration of Cu2+ was obtained by using a color pickup software of the smartphone (R2 = 0.9979). Similarly, the paper discolored from green to red upon TC detection under the ultraviolet lamp of 365 nm. The concentration of TC was linearly correlated with the R/G ratio (R2 = 0.9961). The above results indicated that the low-cost paper-based sensor provided favorable conditions for the daily detection of Cu2+ and TC.

Moreover, hydrogel kit-based sensors were fabricated to improve the portability and sensitivity of the sensor (Fig. 4c). The permeability and colorlessness of hydrogel are conducive to the demonstration of color changes. Under a 254 nm ultraviolet lamp, the color of the hydrogel kit shifted from red to yellow and then to green, as the concentration of Cu2+ varies from 0 to 18 μM. With the aid of color pickup software in the smartphone, the G/R values can be obtained, and good linear relationship between the G/R values with the concentration of Cu2+ was obtained (R2 = 0.9960) (Fig. S8). When detecting TC, the color of the hydrogel kit changed from green to orange and then to red under the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. The R/G value exhibits an excellent linear relationship with the TC concentration (R2 = 0.9980) (Fig. S8).

Notably, two types of hydrogel kits were designed to meet different detection scenarios. The individual hydrogel kits are portable and easy to transport, while the well plate-type hydrogel kits allow for high throughput simultaneous detection of multiple samples, which hold good promise and potential for fast, and on-site detection of Cu2+ and TC in aquatic ecosystems and food samples.

A bifunctional FL nanoprobe Eu-CDs@Eu-MOF was elaborately tailored for dual-channel cross-interference-free determination of TC and Cu2+ simultaneously. The nanoprobe manifested ultra-high sensitivity, rapid response time, good optical stability, excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability in practical detection. The significant dosage-sensitive discoloration of the fluorescent probes enabled the intelligent visual assay. With the assistance of color pickup software in smartphone, portable paper- and hydrogel kit-based nanosensor were constructed for facile, on-site and visualization detection of TC and Cu2+.

Yingqi Yuan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lina Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jun Xu: Visualization, Methodology. Xinna Zhao: Validation, Methodology. Ning Bi: Visualization, Validation. Yongxin Li: Formal analysis, Data curation. Lei Jia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (242300421247), the Science and Technology Innovation Team in Henan Universities (24IRTSTHN014), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51773052, U1904168, 21805071), and the Key Scientific Research Project of Henan Province (24A150011).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112723.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
